prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that the first version of the letter was

choices.text: written to confess how far from home he felt
choices.rationale: Confessing his homesickness describes the later realization, not the cheerful first draft.

choices.text: more cheerful than Tomas actually felt
choices.rationale: Correct. He was cheerful 'as if homesickness were a thing he had outgrown,' though he had not.

choices.text: the version he ultimately decided to send
choices.rationale: He sent none of the versions.

choices.text: an honest account of his deep homesickness
choices.rationale: The honest account was the second version, not the first.

explanation: The first draft put on cheerfulness he did not truly feel.

stimulus.title: The Unsent Letter
stimulus.text: Tomas wrote the letter three times before he stopped trying to send it. In the first version he was cheerful, listing the small triumphs of his new city as if homesickness were a thing he had outgrown. In the second he was honest, and the page filled with everything he missed until it embarrassed him. The third version said almost nothing at all. He kept that one folded in a drawer. Months later he found it again and realized the words he could not send had still done their work: writing them had let him admit, if only to himself, how far from home he truly felt.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that draining and filling marshes most likely left coastal communities

choices.text: better protected from rising seas
choices.rationale: Draining removed the buffer, reducing protection from the sea.

choices.text: more exposed to the force of storms
choices.rationale: Correct. Since marshes blunt storms, removing them would increase exposure.

choices.text: surrounded by thicker marsh grasses
choices.rationale: Filling marshes destroys grasses rather than thickening them.

choices.text: wealthier from the carbon they stored
choices.rationale: Draining released, rather than profited from, stored carbon.

explanation: Removing the marsh buffer would leave communities more exposed to storms.

stimulus.title: The Quiet Buffer
stimulus.text: Where the land meets the sea, a salt marsh can look like wasteland: flat, muddy, and crossed by winding channels. Yet this drab landscape does remarkable work. Its dense grasses slow incoming waves, blunting the force of storms before they reach homes inland. The same grasses trap sediment, letting the marsh build itself upward as the sea slowly rises. Beneath the mud, roots store carbon that might otherwise warm the air. For years, marshes were drained and filled without much thought. Only later did people recognize that the unglamorous marsh had been protecting them all along, asking nothing in return.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that, before zero, writing large numbers clearly was

choices.text: easier than it became afterward
choices.rationale: The passage implies the opposite: zero made writing easier.

choices.text: completely impossible in every case
choices.rationale: People still wrote numbers before zero, so it was not impossible.

choices.text: unaffected by the lack of a zero symbol
choices.rationale: The passage shows the lack of zero did affect clear writing.

choices.text: more difficult than it became afterward
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says zero let differences like 5 and 50 be written clearly.

explanation: Without zero, distinguishing figures like 5 and 50 was harder.

stimulus.title: The Number That Was Nothing
stimulus.text: For much of history, there was no symbol for nothing. People counted sheep and coins easily enough, but the idea of writing down an empty amount seemed pointless, even unsettling. The breakthrough came when mathematicians treated zero not as the absence of a number but as a number in its own right, one that could be added, subtracted, and used to hold a place in larger figures. With zero, the difference between 5 and 50 could be written clearly, and long calculations grew far simpler. A concept that once felt like a riddle became the quiet hinge on which much of modern mathematics turns.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that the delay between a bat's call and its echo tells the bat about an object's

choices.text: color
choices.rationale: Color is never linked to the echoes.

choices.text: temperature
choices.rationale: Temperature is not mentioned.

choices.text: distance
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says the delay lets the bat judge how far away an object is.

choices.text: weight
choices.rationale: Weight is not part of what the echo reveals.

explanation: The call-to-echo delay corresponds to distance.

stimulus.title: Seeing With Sound
stimulus.text: A bat hunting in pitch darkness does not bump into things. It calls out, often at pitches too high for humans to hear, and listens for the echoes that bounce back. From the delay between call and echo, the bat judges how far away an object is; from subtle shifts in the returning sound, it can tell whether the object is moving toward it or away. A moth's fluttering wing returns a different echo than a still leaf. So precise is this sense that some bats can snatch an insect from the air in total darkness. The world the bat perceives is built entirely of sound, a picture painted with echoes.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that merchants spread tall tales about spices in order to

choices.text: encourage as many new sailors as possible to enter the trade
choices.rationale: Tales discouraged rivals rather than recruiting newcomers.

choices.text: keep competitors away from their profitable trade routes
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says the tales were meant 'to discourage rivals.'

choices.text: lower the prices that buyers were willing to pay
choices.rationale: High prices, not lower ones, served the merchants' interest.

choices.text: teach customers the correct way to cook with spices
choices.rationale: The tales were about harvesting, not cooking instruction.

explanation: The tales served to protect routes by discouraging competitors.

stimulus.title: The Price of Pepper
stimulus.text: There was a time when a handful of black pepper could rival a handful of silver. The spice grew only in distant lands, and by the time it reached a European market it had passed through many hands, each adding a markup. Merchants guarded their routes jealously and spread tall tales about how spices were harvested, partly to discourage rivals. The high prices drove sailors to seek their own sea paths to the source, voyages that redrew the map of the world. In the end, the very abundance those voyages produced made spices ordinary, and the fortunes built on rarity quietly dissolved.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that, without lichens, a bare rock would

choices.text: take much longer to support mosses and small plants
choices.rationale: Correct. Since lichens form the first soil, their absence would slow later plant arrival.

choices.text: be colonized first by large trees instead of by mosses
choices.rationale: Large trees colonizing first contradicts the described order.

choices.text: remain forever incapable of supporting any plant life
choices.rationale: The passage does not say plant life would be forever impossible.

choices.text: produce richer soil than rock that lichens have colonized
choices.rationale: Lichen-free rock would have less, not richer, soil.

explanation: Because lichens create the first soil, their absence would delay later colonizers.

stimulus.title: First on the Rock
stimulus.text: When a bare rock is left exposed, the first life to arrive is often a lichen, a strange partnership of fungus and alga living as one. The fungus grips the stone and gathers moisture; the alga, sheltered inside, makes food from sunlight. Together they need no soil at all. As a lichen grows, it slowly releases acids that crack the rock surface, and its dead tissue mixes with grit to form the first thin crumbs of soil. Years later, mosses and small plants can take root in that beginning. The lichen will likely be crowded out by the very community it made possible, an unsung founder of the green world that follows.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that before locks existed, inland water travel was

choices.text: carried out mostly by very large ocean ships
choices.rationale: Ocean ships are never mentioned.

choices.text: faster than any later canal-based travel
choices.rationale: No claim is made that earlier travel was faster.

choices.text: preferred by traders over every overland route
choices.rationale: Trader preferences for routes are not discussed.

choices.text: limited by differences in a river's elevation
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says elevation 'blocked boats from traveling far inland.'

explanation: The opening establishes elevation as the obstacle locks later removed.

stimulus.title: Stairs for Boats
stimulus.text: Rivers do not run level, and for centuries that simple fact blocked boats from traveling far inland. The lock changed things. A lock is a chamber with gates at each end; a boat enters, the gates close, and water is let in or drained out until the boat rises or falls to the next stretch of river. String several locks together, and a vessel can climb a hillside the way a person climbs a staircase. The engineering is humble, relying on nothing more exotic than gravity and patience, yet it knit distant towns into trading partners and turned shallow rivers into highways of commerce.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that the coral record is valuable mainly because it

choices.text: remains perfectly readable without any special training
choices.rationale: The passage calls reading it a challenge requiring effort.

choices.text: preserves data from times before people kept measurements
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage stresses conditions recorded 'before anyone thought to measure them.'

choices.text: can be created quickly in a laboratory whenever needed
choices.rationale: Coral grows over centuries; it is not made quickly in a lab.

choices.text: shows exactly which species of fish lived nearby each year
choices.rationale: Nearby fish species are never mentioned.

explanation: Its value lies in capturing conditions from before instrument records existed.

stimulus.title: The Coral That Keeps Time
stimulus.text: A coral skeleton grows in fine bands, much like the rings of a tree. In bright, warm months the coral lays down thick layers; in cooler, dimmer months the layers thin. By slicing a coral and counting these bands, researchers can read a record of past seasons stretching back centuries. Even better, the chemistry trapped in each band hints at the water's temperature when it formed. So a single old coral can serve as a kind of diary, written not in words but in stone, recording conditions long before anyone thought to measure them. The challenge is learning to read a language no one designed to be read.
